title: C# SOLID — Paper 10
description: Covers DIP implementation, SRP concepts, SRP refactoring, OCP concepts, OCP refactoring, SOLID Real C# Scenarios, Dependency Injection Patterns, Strategy Pattern, Factory & Abstract Factory, and Decorator Pattern.

questions.text: ST20-19: A class `TaxCalculator` contains methods for calculating VAT, sales tax, customs duty, and excise tax — each calling different third-party APIs directly. Which refactoring restores SOLID compliance?

options.text: Move all methods into one static method with a type parameter.
Extract ITaxCalculator per tax type; implement each as a separate class; inject APIs via DI — applying SRP, OCP, and DIP together.
Add more overloads to TaxCalculator.
Seal TaxCalculator to prevent further misuse.

questions.text: ST11-14: In C#, 'decorator registration' in Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection (e.g., using Scrutor's `.Decorate<IService, LoggingDecorator<IService>>()`) demonstrates which patterns together?

options.text: Factory and Singleton
Decorator pattern combined with DI — wrapping a registered service transparently without changing its registration type.
Strategy and Observer
Composite and Proxy

questions.text: ST03-13: Applying OCP to a validation pipeline means:

options.text: Writing all validation in a single large Validate() method.
Defining an IValidator<T> interface; adding new validators as new classes without modifying the pipeline that executes them.
Adding new validation rules by editing the base Validator class.
Using regex for all validation to avoid conditional branches.

questions.text: ST13-13: Which of the following is a real-world .NET example of the Abstract Factory pattern?

options.text: DbProviderFactories.GetFactory(string) — returns a provider-specific factory that creates connections, commands, and parameters for a specific database provider.
Console.WriteLine() — outputting text to the console.
Thread.Sleep() — pausing a thread.
String.Format() — formatting strings.

questions.text: ST01-13: Which of the following classes is most likely SRP-compliant?

options.text: AppManager — manages application lifecycle, logging, configuration, and user sessions.
JwtTokenGenerator — responsible solely for creating and signing JWT tokens.
DataHelper — provides utility methods for database, file, and network operations.
SystemService — handles system-level tasks across all domains.

questions.text: ST14-10: A `TransactionDecorator` wraps an `IRepository` and wraps every method call in a database transaction. This demonstrates the Decorator pattern because:

options.text: TransactionDecorator inherits from the repository base class.
It adds cross-cutting transactional behavior by wrapping an IRepository without modifying the real repository class.
It uses reflection to intercept repository calls.
It replaces the repository interface with a transaction-aware interface.

questions.text: ST04-13: Which of the following is an example of OCP applied at the architectural level?

options.text: Editing the core domain library for each new business requirement.
Designing a plugin architecture where new features ship as separate assemblies that register themselves against defined extension points.
Deploying new features by hotfixing production binaries.
Using feature flags in the core assembly to enable/disable behavior.

questions.text: ST20-20: A developer wrote: `public class OrderService : IOrderService, IEmailService, IReportService`. What SOLID issue does this raise?

options.text: OCP — the class is closed for extension.
SRP — OrderService is implementing the contracts of three unrelated concerns, suggesting it has multiple responsibilities.
LSP — the class cannot be substituted correctly.
DIP — the class should not implement interfaces.

questions.text: ST02-13: 'Vertical slice architecture' is a modern approach to SRP at the feature level. It means:

options.text: Organising code by technical layer (controllers, services, repositories).
Organising code by feature, with each feature containing all its own layers (handler, validator, query, command), giving each feature a single reason to change.
Splitting the database schema vertically by entity.
Keeping all feature code in a single file per feature.

questions.text: ST10-12: In ASP.NET Core, which built-in DI extension method chains multiple registrations of the same interface, allowing all implementations to be resolved as IEnumerable<T>?

options.text: services.AddMultiple<T>()
Calling services.AddTransient<IService, Impl1>() and services.AddTransient<IService, Impl2>() — resolving IEnumerable<IService> returns both.
services.AddCollection<T>()
services.AddAll<T>()

questions.text: ST12-13: A `CompressionContext` supports different compression algorithms. Which of the following shows the correct usage pattern in C#?

options.text: context.Algorithm = "gzip"; context.Compress(data);
context.SetStrategy(new GzipStrategy()); context.ExecuteCompression(data);
new GzipContext(data).Compress();
CompressionContext.Gzip.Compress(data);

questions.text: ST03-14: Which aspect of SOLID does the concept of 'programming to an interface, not an implementation' most directly support?

options.text: SRP — each interface has one responsibility.
Both OCP and DIP — depending on an abstraction (interface) allows behavior to be extended and substituted without modifying calling code.
LSP — interfaces guarantee substitutability.
ISP — all interfaces must be small.

questions.text: ST14-11: In ASP.NET Core, middleware is registered as `app.Use(next => async ctx => { ... await next(ctx); ... })`. This is structurally equivalent to which pattern?

options.text: Observer — each middleware subscribes to requests.
Decorator / Chain of Responsibility — each middleware wraps the next, adding behavior before and after calling it.
Strategy — each middleware selects a different algorithm.
Factory — each middleware creates a new request context.

questions.text: ST01-14: Consider a `UserAccount` class with fields: Username, PasswordHash, Email, ProfilePicture, LastLoginDate, Address, PhoneNumber — and methods for authentication, profile editing, notification preferences, and billing. From an SRP perspective:

options.text: This is acceptable — it is one entity.
This class aggregates multiple concerns (identity, profile, billing, notifications) and should be decomposed into focused classes.
Adding more fields would improve cohesion.
The class is SRP compliant as long as it maps to one database table.

questions.text: ST11-15: The `IServiceCollection` in ASP.NET Core can be extended with custom extension methods (e.g., `services.AddMyFeature()`). This is an example of which principle?

options.text: LSP — services are substitutable.
OCP — IServiceCollection is extended without modification by adding new extension methods.
ISP — the service collection is kept small.
DIP — the extension method depends on an abstraction.

questions.text: ST04-14: A developer applied OCP to a pricing engine using a list of `IPricingRule` objects. New business rules are added by implementing IPricingRule. What happens to the existing PricingEngine class?

options.text: It must be modified to include the new rule.
It remains unchanged — the new IPricingRule implementation is registered and the engine executes it without modification.
It must be recompiled to pick up the new rule.
It is replaced by a new PricingEngine version.

questions.text: ST13-14: In the .NET ecosystem, `ILoggerFactory` and `ILogger<T>` represent which pattern?

options.text: Abstract Factory — ILoggerFactory creates ILogger<T> instances configured for specific categories.
Singleton — only one ILogger<T> exists per type.
Observer — ILogger<T> subscribes to log events.
Strategy — different log levels select different algorithms.

questions.text: ST20-21: Given: `public class Validator { public bool IsValid(object obj) { if (obj is Customer c) return ValidateCustomer(c); if (obj is Order o) return ValidateOrder(o); return false; } }` — what is the primary SOLID issue?

options.text: DIP — Validator depends on concrete types.
OCP — every new type requires editing Validator; fix by using a generic IValidator<T> or a dictionary of validators.
ISP — Validator has too many methods.
LSP — Customer and Order break substitution.

questions.text: ST10-13: In Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, `ValidateOnBuild()` (available in .NET 6+) does what?

options.text: Runs all unit tests at application startup.
Validates that all registered services can be resolved (detects missing registrations and captive dependencies) at build time, preventing runtime DI failures.
Validates that services implement their declared interfaces.
Ensures all services are singleton.

questions.text: ST02-14: A developer has a `ReportService` with 10 public methods. Five relate to data fetching, three to formatting, and two to delivery. The SRP refactoring produces:

options.text: One class with 10 private methods grouped by region.
Three classes: ReportDataService, ReportFormatter, and ReportDeliveryService — each with a single focused responsibility.
Ten classes — one per method.
Two classes splitting data-fetching from everything else.

questions.text: ST03-15: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between OCP and the Template Method pattern?

options.text: Template Method violates OCP by requiring modification of the base class for each new variant.
Template Method supports OCP by defining a stable algorithm skeleton (closed for modification) with override points (open for extension via subclasses).
Template Method is only used with abstract classes and has no relation to OCP.
OCP and Template Method are in direct conflict.

questions.text: ST14-12: When using the Decorator pattern to add retry logic to an `IHttpService`, the `RetryHttpService` decorator should:

options.text: Inherit from the concrete HttpService implementation.
Implement IHttpService and hold a reference to another IHttpService; on failure, retry by calling the inner service again.
Replace IHttpService entirely with a RetryHttpService interface.
Use static methods to retry calls to the real HttpService.

questions.text: ST12-14: A C# `IBillingStrategy` has `decimal Calculate(Order order)`. Two implementations exist: `FlatRateBilling` and `PerUsageBilling`. New requirement: add `SubscriptionBilling`. What is needed?

options.text: Modify IBillingStrategy to add a SubscriptionCalculate method.
Add a `SubscriptionBilling : IBillingStrategy` class implementing `Calculate(Order order)` — no existing code changes needed.
Add a case to a switch statement in the billing engine.
Modify FlatRateBilling to handle subscription logic.

questions.text: ST01-15: When applying SRP, the 'actor' concept (from Clean Architecture) maps to a 'reason to change' because:

options.text: Actors write the code and introduce bugs.
Different business actors (e.g., Finance vs. HR) drive different change requests; if a class serves multiple actors, it has multiple reasons to change.
Actors are only relevant in theatre, not software.
Actors represent classes in UML diagrams.

questions.text: ST11-16: Which of the following describes 'ambient context' as an alternative dependency access pattern, and why it is discouraged?

options.text: Ambient context uses static properties/thread-local state to provide dependencies; it is discouraged because it hides dependencies, makes testing harder, and breaks explicit DIP contracts.
Ambient context is a recommended pattern for sharing state across services.
Ambient context is used only in ASP.NET Core and is fully supported.
Ambient context is equivalent to constructor injection.

questions.text: ST13-15: A test project uses a `MockRepositoryFactory` to create `InMemoryRepository<T>` objects for each entity type. This is applying which pattern?

options.text: Singleton — reusing the same repository.
Abstract Factory — the factory creates a family of related test doubles (repositories) for use together in integration tests.
Builder — constructing repositories step by step.
Prototype — cloning existing repository instances.

questions.text: ST04-15: In C# source generators, a generator produces additional code at compile time without modifying existing source files. This is an example of OCP because:

options.text: Source generators modify existing classes at runtime.
New behavior is added (generated) without editing the original source files, which remain closed for modification.
Source generators are a form of inheritance.
Source generators violate OCP by adding code automatically.

questions.text: ST20-22: A method `ProcessRequest(HttpRequest req)` has 200 lines and handles authentication, routing, business logic, and response serialization. Which principles are violated?

options.text: Only OCP — it cannot be extended.
SRP (multiple responsibilities), OCP (cannot add behavior without modification), and DIP (likely depends on concretions).
Only ISP — the method signature accepts too broad a type.
Only LSP — the method breaks substitution.

questions.text: ST02-15: In Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters), 'ports' represent which SRP/DIP concept?

options.text: Concrete implementations of external services.
Interface abstractions (driven by or driving the domain) that define single-responsibility contracts, allowing adapters (implementations) to be swapped without changing the domain.
Network ports for inter-service communication.
Database connection pools.

questions.text: ST10-14: Which of the following is a sign of a well-configured DI container in a production ASP.NET Core application?

options.text: All services registered as singletons for maximum performance.
Each service registered with the appropriate lifetime (transient/scoped/singleton), validated at startup, with no captive dependencies.
No interfaces used — only concrete types registered.
The Service Locator pattern used throughout the application.
